en.extract:
The chestnut-backed buttonquail (Turnix castanotus) is a species of bird in the family Turnicidae. It is endemic to Australia.


Taxonomy
John Gould described the species in 1840, from a specimen collected by Benjamin Bynoe, ship's surgeon of HMS Beagle, from northwestern Australia, as Hemipodius castanotus. English zoologist George Gray placed it in the genus Turnix in 1870. William Robert Ogilvie-Grant corrected the name to Turnix castanonota in 1889. Three subspecies were described by Gregory Mathews, but all are now considered as invalid and merely individual variants.


Etymology
"Chestnut-backed buttonquail" has been designated the official name by the International Ornithologists' Union (IOC). The species name is derived from the Ancient Greek words kastanon "chestnut" and noton "back". Gould called it "chestnut-backed hemipode" in 1848, noting the colonists called it "thick-billed quail". The buttonquail species were generally known as "quail" (hence "chestnut-backed quail" ) until the RAOU promoted the current usage of "buttonquail" in 1978, which was then universally adopted.


Distribution and habitat
Native to northern Australia, the species ranges from the Dampier Peninsula in Western Australia across to Westmoreland Station in the Gulf Country of northwestern Queensland, with possible records from Gregory Downs and Augustus Downs further east. It is also native to Melville Island and Groote Eylandt. It was common around Borroloola and McArthur River, but not currently. Its existence in Queensland was only confirmed in 2020.
Its natural habitats are low grasslands and Eucalyptus forests and woodland, favouring quartzite-sandstone ridges, plateaus and escarpments, mostly (but not always) in areas that receive over 800 mm (31 in) summer rainfall.


Behaviour
The usual sex roles are reversed in the buttonquail genus (Turnix), as the larger and more brightly-coloured female mates with multiple male partners and leaves them to incubate the eggs. The female lays a clutch of 1–5 (usually 4) pyriform eggs, which are glossy-white with sparse small dark blotches.


References

The '''chestnut-backed buttonquail''' ('''''Turnix castanotus''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Turnicidae]]. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[Australia]].

==Taxonomy==
[[John Gould]] described the species in 1840, from a specimen collected by [[Benjamin Bynoe]], ship's surgeon of {{HMS|Beagle}}, from northwestern Australia, as ''Hemipodius castanotus''.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Gould |first1=John |title=. Letter to Chairman of the Scientific Committee, Zoological Society of London, read before meeting of the Society of Oct. 8, 1839 |journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London |date=1840 |volume=7 |pages=139–145 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30571382}}</ref> English zoologist [[George Robert Gray|George Gray]] placed it in the genus ''[[Turnix]]'' in 1870.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Gray |first1=G.R. |title=Hand-list of genera and species of birds, distinguishing those contained in the British Museum |date=1870 |publisher=British Museum |location=London |page=270 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/8388909}}</ref> [[William Robert Ogilvie-Grant]] corrected the name to ''Turnix castanonota'' in 1889.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Ogilvie-Grant |first1=William Robert |title=On the Genus Turnix |journal=Ibis |date=1889 |volume=31 |issue=4 |pages=446–475 [453] |doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1889.tb06396.x |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/16280833}}</ref> Three subspecies were described by Gregory Mathews,<ref name="AFD"/> but all are now considered as invalid and merely individual variants.<ref name="McGowan 2010"/>

==Etymology==
"Chestnut-backed buttonquail" has been designated the official name by the [[International Ornithologists' Union]] (IOC).<ref name="ioclist">{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | year=2021| title= Buttonquail, thick-knees, sheathbills, plovers, oystercatchers, stilts, painted-snipes, jacanas, Plains-wanderer, seedsnipes | work=World Bird List Version 12.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/buttonquail/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=9 April 2022 }}</ref> The species name is derived from the [[Ancient Greek]] words ''kastanon'' "chestnut" and ''noton'' "back".<ref name="fraser19">{{cite book |last1=Gray |first1=Jeannie |last2=Fraser |first2=Ian |title=Australian Bird Names: Origins and Meanings  |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=aTytDwAAQBAJ |year=2019 |publisher=Csiro Publishing |location=Collingwood, Victoria |isbn=978-1-486-31164-4 |pages=50–51}}</ref> Gould called it "chestnut-backed hemipode" in 1848, noting the colonists called it "thick-billed quail". The buttonquail species were generally known as "quail" (hence "chestnut-backed quail" ) until the RAOU promoted the current usage of "buttonquail" in 1978, which was then universally adopted.<ref name="fraser19"/>

==Distribution and habitat==
Native to northern Australia, the species ranges from the [[Dampier Peninsula]] in Western Australia across to Westmoreland Station in the [[Gulf Country]] of northwestern Queensland, with possible records from [[Gregory, Queensland|Gregory Downs]] and [[Augustus Downs Station|Augustus Downs]] further east. It is also native to [[Melville Island (Australia)|Melville Island]] and [[Groote Eylandt]]. It was common around [[Borroloola]] and [[McArthur River]], but not currently. Its existence in Queensland was only confirmed in 2020.<ref name=webster2021>{{cite journal |last1=Webster |first1=Patrick T.D. |last2=Stoetzel |first2=Henry J. |title=First confirmed record of Chestnut-backed Button-quail ''Turnix castanotus'' in Queensland |journal=Australian Field Ornithology |date=2021 |volume=38 |pages=145–150 |doi=10.20938/afo38145150|s2cid=238874838 |doi-access=free }}</ref><!-- cites 2 previous sentences -->

Its natural [[habitat]]s are low grasslands and ''[[Eucalyptus]]'' forests and woodland, favouring quartzite-sandstone ridges, plateaus and escarpments, mostly (but not always) in areas that receive over {{cvt|800|mm}} summer rainfall.<ref name="McGowan 2010">{{cite book|last=McGowan|first=Phil|author2=Madge, Steve|title=Pheasants, Partridges & Grouse: Including buttonquails, sandgrouse and allies|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|location=London, United Kingdom|date=2010|orig-date=2002|pages=430–431|isbn=978-1-4081-3565-5|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WffUBAAAQBAJ&pg=PA430}}</ref>

==Behaviour==
The usual sex roles are reversed in the buttonquail genus (''[[Turnix]]''), as the larger and more brightly-coloured female mates with [[Polyandry|multiple male]] partners and leaves them to [[Avian incubation|incubate]] the eggs.<ref>{{cite book |title=Every Australian Bird Illustrated|editor=Wade Peter |year=1977 |pages=78–79|publisher=Rigby|isbn=0-7270-0009-8}}</ref> The female lays a clutch of 1–5 (usually 4) pyriform eggs, which are glossy-white with sparse small dark blotches.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.20938/afo39012018|title=Nests and eggs of the Chestnut-backed Button-quail Turnix castanotus: Two new nests and a review of previous descriptions|year=2022|last1=Webster|first1=Patrick|last2=Jackett|first2=Nigel|last3=Mason|first3=Ian|last4=Rush|first4=Emily|last5=Leseberg|first5=Nicholas|last6=Watson|first6=James|journal=Australian Field Ornithology|volume=39|pages=12–18|s2cid=246895590|doi-access=free}}</ref>
